era_context: Ur III dynasty dominates Mesopotamia; Egyptian First Intermediate Period; Indus Valley Civilization mature phase; Early Bronze Age in the Aegean and Anatolia; traditional early Xia Dynasty period in China.

events.title: Amar-Sin's reign continues with administrative consolidation
events.description: Amar-Sin's reign continues with extensive administrative consolidation. The Ur III bureaucracy reaches its peak efficiency, with detailed cuneiform records documenting taxation, labor organization, temple management, and military logistics. The standardization of weights, measures, and administrative procedures across the Sumerian city-states represents a remarkable achievement in ancient governance. Amar-Sin also undertakes religious duties, including the sacred marriage ceremony (hieros gamos) with the goddess Inanna.
events.certainty_note: Amar-Sin's reign is extensively documented in contemporary cuneiform records.

events.title: Indus Valley Civilization: Peak urban development
events.description: The Indus Valley Civilization reaches its peak in urban development and population. Major cities feature sophisticated water management systems, standardized brick sizes, and planned street layouts. Archaeological evidence indicates a high degree of social organization and economic specialization. The civilization's undeciphered script appears on seals, pottery, and other artifacts, suggesting a complex administrative and possibly religious system.

sources.name: Archaeological excavations at Indus Valley sites

events.certainty_note: The mature Indus Valley Civilization is archaeologically well-attested.

events.title: Egyptian First Intermediate Period: Continued regional competition
events.description: Egypt remains fragmented during the First Intermediate Period, with the Ninth and Tenth Dynasties ruling from Herakleopolis in Lower Egypt and the Eleventh Dynasty consolidating power in Upper Egypt. Regional governors (nomarchs) exercise considerable autonomy. This period is characterized by reduced monumental construction and increased local artistic and literary production. The political fragmentation will eventually be resolved by the Eleventh Dynasty's expansion northward.

sources.name: Egyptian administrative records and tomb inscriptions

events.certainty_note: The First Intermediate Period is documented in Egyptian sources, but absolute dating is uncertain (±50 years).

disconfirming_evidence: No specific disconfirming evidence for events listed. The chronological framework remains contested by some scholars who propose alternative chronologies.
historiographic_note: This year is essentially continuous with -2210 and -2211. The year-by-year approach becomes increasingly artificial in the ancient world, where major developments span decades or centuries. The historical record remains heavily skewed toward Mesopotamia and Egypt.
